blurb: An intensely clove-and-jasmine-scented garden pink: fringed white flowers each centered on a raspberry-pink eye, hovering over a tight blue-green grassy mat. Cool-spring bloom on a tough, deer-proof, drought-tolerant edging plant for the Front Range.
sun: Full sun to light afternoon shade
water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established, needs sharp drainage
spread: Clumping; forms a slowly spreading evergreen mat
seasons: Evergreen blue-green grassy foliage year-round; fragrant white-and-pink flowers late spring into summer; semi-evergreen mat in winter
wildlife: Fragrant flowers draw butterflies, bees, and other pollinators
toxic: Mildly toxic to cats, dogs, and horses (foliage can cause mild stomach upset; sap may irritate skin)

care.hardiness: Hardy across the Front Range — garden pinks of this type are reliable in USDA zones 4–9, so it overwinters easily here (Boulder is ~5b–6a). The 'Scent First' (Whetman) hybrid pinks were bred for strong fragrance, repeat bloom, and good vigor; the limiting factor in our climate is drainage, not cold — the evergreen mat sulks and rots in soggy winter soil.[1][2]
care.planting: Set out nursery plants in spring after the mid-May average last frost, or in early fall, into gritty, sharply drained soil — a raised edge, rock garden, or wall top is ideal. Plant the crown at or just above soil level; burying it invites rot. In containers use a fast-draining mix and don't let the pot stay wet; potted crowns are far less cold-hardy than in-ground ones, so overwinter pots in a sheltered, dry spot.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun — six-plus hours — for the tightest mat and the most flowers; light afternoon shade is tolerated and can help in Boulder's hottest stretches. Too much shade makes it loose, floppy, and shy to bloom.[1][2]
care.soil: Lean, gritty, well-drained, near-neutral to slightly alkaline soil suits it perfectly — Colorado's alkaline ground is a natural fit. Sharp drainage is essential; heavy, wet clay rots the crown. Amend heavy soil with grit rather than rich compost, which softens the mat.[1][2]
care.water: Low to moderate. Water to establish the first season, then water deeply but infrequently — it is drought-tolerant once settled and resents soggy ground. Avoid overhead watering, which can spot the foliage and rot the dense mat; keep water off the crown in winter.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space plants about 8–12 in apart to knit into a continuous edging mat; each clump stays low (6–10 in in bloom) and spreads slowly to 10–14 in wide.[1][2]
care.propagation: A named hybrid, so propagate vegetatively to keep it true: take 2–3 in non-flowering tip cuttings ('pipings') in early summer, or layer/divide established mats in spring or early fall. Seed of a hybrid won't come true.[2]
care.bloom: Fragrant fringed white flowers with a raspberry-pink eye, mostly late spring into early summer, with scattered repeat bloom if deadheaded — shear spent flowers off to encourage another flush and keep the mat tidy.[1][2]
care.feeding: Light. A little balanced slow-release fertilizer in spring is enough; lean soil and modest feeding keep the mat compact and free-flowering. Avoid heavy nitrogen, which produces lush foliage and few flowers.[1]
care.maintenance: Deadhead/shear spent flower stems to prolong bloom and neaten the plant. The evergreen mat needs little else; tidy any winter-burned foliage in early spring. Divide or re-layer every few years when the center thins to keep the clump vigorous.[1][2]
care.troubles: Generally trouble-free in a sunny, sharply drained spot. The main risks are crown and root rot in wet or poorly drained soil and fungal leaf spot/rust in crowded, humid, overhead-watered plantings — good airflow, lean soil, and water at the base prevent both.[1][2]
care.harvest: If you grow it for the kitchen, snip fully open, unsprayed flowers in the cool of the morning when the scent is strongest, and use the petals fresh — pull each petal from the calyx and trim away the bitter pale heel at its base before using.[3]

edible.summary: The flower petals are edible with a sweet, spicy clove flavor — but only the colored petal, with the bitter white heel removed, and only from unsprayed plants.[2][3]
edible.parts: Only the flower petals are used. Garden pinks (Dianthus) have long been a culinary edible flower — the petals carry a sweet, clove-to-jasmine spice that flavors sugars, syrups, vinegars, cordials, and desserts, or garnishes salads, cakes, and drinks. The rest of the plant (leaves, stems, roots) is not eaten.[2][3]
edible.preparation: Pull the colored petals from the calyx and snip or pinch off the narrow pale 'heel' at the base of each petal — it is bitter and should be discarded. Use the trimmed petals fresh: scatter on dishes, crystallize with sugar, or steep to flavor syrups and drinks. Only ever eat petals from plants you know are unsprayed (no pesticide/herbicide residues); never use florist's carnations.[2][3]
edible.caution: Eat only the petal, with the bitter white base removed, and only from your own unsprayed garden plants — commercially grown Dianthus may carry pesticide residues and is not for eating. The foliage and sap are a separate matter: the leaves and stems are rated mildly toxic to cats, dogs, and horses (ingestion can cause mild gastrointestinal upset) and the sap can irritate sensitive skin, so the leaves/stems are not a food. Keep the edible use to clearly identified, unsprayed flower petals.[2]

gaps: Photos are species-representative garden/cottage pinks (Dianthus plumarius and kin — fringed, fragrant, blue-green grassy mats) — no open-licensed photo of the named 'Scent First Jasmine' cultivar exists. That cultivar is a fragrant white flower with a raspberry-pink eye; the shown pinks match its mat, fringed petals, and habit, with some variation in petal color.
